author: [{'author': 'The name of the individual who authored the work, typically presented in a formal, centered layout on the title page of an academic document. It is positioned directly below the title and above the work\'s description (e.g., "A Dissertation"), and is preceded by the word "By" and followed by academic credentials (e.g., "Doctor of Philosophy") and an institutional affiliation (e.g., "University of California, Berkeley"). The name is usually printed in a bold, serif font and is distinct from other textual elements such as the title, chapter headings, or section titles due to its specific placement, accompanying academic information, and formal presentation. This definition excludes names that appear in the body of the text, footnotes, endnotes, annotations, or navigational elements such as tables of contents, lists of figures, or lists of tables, as well as names in bibliographic entries (e.g., reference lists), which may include names but lack the associated academic credentials and institutional affiliation and are not presented in the formal, centered layout of a title page. It also excludes titles or section headers that may appear in non-academic documents, such as appendices or meeting agendas, even if they are prominently displayed, and specifically excludes table titles or other labels that are part of a table or figure, as they do not contain academic credentials or institutional affiliation and are not positioned in the formal author block on a title page.'}]
chapter: [{'chapter': 'Section headings that serve as structural markers within the main body of a document, typically positioned at the top of a page and immediately preceding the main body of text for a new section. These headings are formatted in a bold, capitalized font and are explicitly labeled with the word "CHAPTER" (in any case) followed by a Roman numeral or Arabic numeral (e.g., "CHAPTER I", "Chapter 4") and a descriptive title. This specific label distinguishes them from all other section headings, including numbered subsections (e.g., "1.2", "2.1") and technical section titles. Chapter headings are exclusive to the main narrative structure and do not appear in supplementary sections such as lists, appendices, footnotes, proofs, technical code blocks, or other front matter elements. They are also not used for labeling mathematical equations or other technical content. Chapter headings are not found within the body of a chapter or section, nor are they used for subsections or subheadings. They are distinct from tables, figures, and other data presentation elements, and are not found in navigational elements such as tables of contents. The combination of the "CHAPTER" label (in any case), bold and capitalized formatting, their position at the start of a new section, and their role in demarcating major sections within the main narrative structure is the definitive characteristic that distinguishes them from all other text in the document.'}]
equation: [{'equation': 'A mathematical equation is a self-contained expression that defines a relationship between variables or quantities using a combination of standard mathematical symbols, functions, and operations. It is structured with a clear left-hand side, typically a single variable or expression, followed by an equals sign and a more complex right-hand side. The equation is presented in a formal, typeset style, often using a serif font, and is embedded within a context that explains the variables and terms used. It must represent a formal mathematical relationship and not merely a sequence of characters, a title, label, or heading. The expression must include variables and operations, and it must be a valid mathematical statement, not a reference, identifier, or non-mathematical text. It is distinct from programming code, narrative text, tables of data, or numbered lists of textual entries, such as section titles or table of contents items, even if they contain mathematical symbols or numbers. The expression must be a complete and self-contained mathematical statement, not a collection of mathematical objects or a list of expressions, unless explicitly defined as a single equation or relationship. It must not be a numerical label or reference in parentheses used to identify an equation, as such labels are not part of the mathematical expression itself but serve as identifiers. The expression must not be a block of narrative text that contains numbers, scientific notation, or mathematical symbols, as these are used to support a description or argument rather than to define a formal mathematical relationship.'}]
equation number: [{'equation number': 'A self-contained mathematical expression, typically involving variables, functions, operators, and constants, formatted as a distinct block of text, which is explicitly labeled with a unique alphanumeric identifier enclosed in square brackets and positioned immediately to its right. This label serves as a formal reference point for the expression within the document, and the expression itself must be a complex mathematical formula, not a simple text label, identifier, or structural element such as a heading, table reference, or list item. The expression should be presented in a mathematical context, such as a scientific or technical document, and should not resemble code or programming syntax. The label must be directly associated with the mathematical expression and not stand alone as a simple numerical reference or be part of a textual footnote, narrative, or citation list. The label must be enclosed in square brackets and must not be part of a textual list or descriptive sequence, such as a bibliography or reference list. The label must not be a structural element of the document, such as a section or subsection heading, nor a part of a numbered list or a reference to a figure or table. The label must not be part of a table of contents, list of figures, or list of tables, and the expression must not be a simple numerical reference or a structural element of the document. The expression must be isolated from surrounding text and not embedded within a paragraph of prose.'}]
figure: [{'figure': 'A standalone visual representation, such as a photograph, diagram, image, chart, graph, or set of plots, integrated into a document to illustrate a specific experimental result, observation, or conceptual model. It is labeled with a figure number and a descriptive caption, and is designed to convey information visually, often supporting the surrounding text. This definition excludes tabular data, textual lists, indexes, and any structured textual content that references figures, focusing only on visual elements that depict real-world observations, experimental results, or conceptual models, and explicitly excludes structured data presentations such as tables.'}]
figure caption: [{'figure caption': 'A textual element located directly beneath a figure, serving as a descriptive label. It begins with a figure label (e.g., "Figure X.Y") followed by a detailed explanation of the visual content, including components, experimental conditions, and key findings. It is formatted as a single, continuous block of text, written in a clear, concise, and formal style, and is distinct from other textual elements such as section headings, author names, or footnotes. The caption is specifically tied to the figure it describes and is not used to introduce a new section or chapter. It does not contain mathematical equations or formulae, and its primary function is to describe the visual content of the figure rather than to present analytical or theoretical expressions. The caption should not be a standalone mathematical expression or a component of a larger paragraph. It is not marked by a superscript number and is not a footnote or supplementary note. The caption is always associated with a specific figure and is not a structural element of the document like a section title or list of tables. Additionally, the caption is not part of a list or a table of contents and is not a structural element of the document. The caption is not a reference list entry and does not contain bibliographic information such as author names, publication years, or journal titles. The caption does not discuss scientific findings, experimental results, or technical details beyond what is directly related to the visual content of the figure. The caption is not located directly above a table or any other tabular structure. The caption is not a title, section heading, or author name, and it is not a structural element of the document.'}]
footnote: [{'footnote': 'A textual element located at the bottom of a page, separated from the main text by a horizontal line, that functions as a citation or reference to an external source. It begins with a unique, superscripted number corresponding to a reference point in the main text and contains a single, self-contained paragraph providing a citation, a direct quote, or an explanatory comment. Its primary purpose is to offer supplementary information, such as a source for a claim, a definition, or a detailed explanation, without interrupting the flow of the main argument. This element is a standard feature of academic and formal writing, used to acknowledge the work of others and to provide a trail for the reader to follow. It is exclusively composed of text and is distinct from non-textual elements such as figures, tables, charts, or graphs, even if those elements are labeled with numbers. It is also distinct from numerical labels or references embedded within the main text, which are not separated by a horizontal line and do not contain self-contained paragraphs. Furthermore, it is distinct from bibliographic reference lists (e.g., "References" or "Bibliography" sections) that may contain numbered entries, as these are not located at the bottom of the page, are not separated by a horizontal line, and do not serve the same supplementary informational purpose. A footnote is a single, self-contained paragraph, whereas a reference list is a structured list of sources. Additionally, a footnote is not a title, heading, or section label, even if it contains a number, and it is not part of a structured document section such as an appendix or a list of discussion questions. It is specifically distinguished from numbered headings or labels within the main body of the text, which may contain numbers but are not separated by a horizontal line and do not provide supplementary information in the same manner. It is also distinct from tables, figures, or any other structured data presentation elements, as it is not composed of rows, columns, or numerical data, and does not serve to present or summarize data. Furthermore, it is distinct from structured lists such as tables of contents, which contain multiple entries with titles and page numbers and are not located at the bottom of the page, are not separated by a horizontal line, and do not serve the same supplementary informational purpose.'}]
list of content heading: [{'list of content heading': 'A structured, multi-entry list serving as a navigational aid within a document, specifically for the main content. Each entry consists of a unique alphanumeric identifier (e.g., "2.1" or "3.1"), a concise descriptive title summarizing a section, table, or concept, and a page number aligned to the far right. The title is separated from the identifier by a series of dots (dot leader) that extend to the page number, creating a clear visual connection. The entries are arranged in a clear, hierarchical, and sequential order, with the identifier indicating the item\'s position in the document. This format provides a comprehensive index of the document\\\'s main content, allowing a reader to quickly locate specific information. The object is distinct from section headings, as it is a list of entries rather than a single title, and it is typically found at the beginning of a document or section to guide navigation. It is also distinct from lists of abbreviations or definitions, as it does not include full forms of terms or mathematical expressions, and it specifically includes page numbers for navigation. The object is further distinguished from figure captions, table captions, or other single-label descriptions, as it is a list of multiple entries with a consistent format and navigational purpose, not a single descriptive label for a figure or table. The object must contain multiple entries and is not a single reference, such as a numbered equation, formula, or citation, which may have a similar identifier but lacks the list structure, titles, page numbers, and dot leaders. It is also distinct from footnotes, endnotes, or other single textual references, even if they contain numbers or citations, as these do not form a navigational list and lack the required structural elements. The titles in the list are concise summaries and do not include detailed explanations, references to components, or narrative descriptions of the content. The presence of dot leaders connecting the title to the page number is a critical feature that distinguishes this object from other lists, such as bibliographies or reference lists, which do not use this formatting. The object must be a list of multiple entries with a consistent format and navigational purpose, and it must not be a single paragraph of narrative text, even if it contains numerical identifiers or page numbers. The object is also distinct from bibliographic reference lists, which contain full citations with author names, publication details, and journal information, and lack dot leaders and page number alignment. Additionally, the object must not be a single heading or title, even if it contains numbers or identifiers, as it lacks the list structure, multiple entries, and dot leaders that define a navigational list. The object is further distinguished from data tables, figures, or other single content units, even if they contain numerical identifiers or titles, as it is not a presentation of data or a single descriptive label but a multi-entry list with a navigational function, and it must include dot leaders and page numbers aligned to the right to connect the title to the page number. The object must only include entries related to the main content of the document, such as tables, figures, or sections, and must not include non-content entries like "References," "Publications," or "Vita" that are typically found at the end of a document. The object is typically introduced by a clear, capitalized title (e.g., "List of Tables") that identifies the content of the list.'}]
list of content text: [{'list of content text': 'A structured, multi-entry list from a formal document such as a thesis or research paper, characterized by entries with numerical identifiers (e.g., "Figure 3.10", "4.1") on the left, descriptive titles in the middle, and corresponding page numbers on the right, all connected by a series of dots (dotted leaders). The list consists of multiple entries arranged vertically, each representing a distinct figure, table, or section in the document, and serves as a navigational aid. This format is distinct from isolated numerical labels (e.g., equation numbers), footnotes, captions, section headings, mathematical equations, or bibliographic lists, which lack the multi-entry structure, dotted leaders, and page numbers. It also excludes single paragraphs or narrative text blocks, even if they contain numerical identifiers, as they lack the required structural elements of a list of content text. The list must be a navigational aid for figures, tables, or sections, and not a reference list, and must include dotted leaders and page numbers. Additionally, it excludes section headings that are part of the main narrative text, even if numbered, as they are not structured as lists and do not serve as navigational aids. Furthermore, it excludes single tables or data presentations, even if they contain numerical identifiers or are titled, as they lack the multi-entry structure and dotted leaders, and do not serve as navigational aids. It also excludes hierarchical lists of section headings and subheadings, even if they contain numerical identifiers and page numbers, as they lack the dotted leaders and are not structured as lists of figures, tables, or sections. Finally, it excludes standalone titles or headings, even if they are part of a formal document, as they lack the multi-entry structure, dotted leaders, and page numbers required for a list of content text. The list may or may not have a formal title such as "List of Figures" or "List of Tables," but it must function as a navigational aid for figures, tables, or sections. The presence of a table structure, even with numerical identifiers, disqualifies an object from being a list of content text. The list may include non-numeric identifiers (e.g., "4.9", "4.10") as long as they follow the same structural pattern of numerical identifiers, descriptive titles, and page numbers connected by dotted leaders.'}]
page number: Numerical indication of the current page.
paragraph: [{'paragraph': 'A textual unit composed of multiple, logically connected sentences that develop a coherent idea, argument, or narrative through continuous prose, functioning as a primary component of the main body of a document. This unit is typographically distinct from figures, captions, section titles, footnotes, and formulaic expressions, and is characterized by natural language, narrative or analytical reasoning, and a structured flow of ideas. It may include references to figures, citations, and technical terminology, and it excludes self-contained mathematical equations, symbolic expressions, or labeled formulae, as well as supplementary textual units such as footnotes, endnotes, or citations, which are semantically and structurally separate from the main narrative and lack the syntactic and semantic continuity of a true paragraph. It also excludes structured lists, itemized entries, or catalog-like text (such as lists of tables or figures), which, despite containing sentences, are organized for reference or navigation and do not exhibit the continuous, flowing discourse of a true paragraph. Furthermore, it excludes text that is formatted as a table of contents, list of sections, or index, characterized by numerical labels, consistent alignment, and a repetitive, navigational structure, as these lack the narrative coherence and continuous flow of a true paragraph. Additionally, it excludes bibliographic reference lists, which are structured collections of citations with consistent formatting (e.g., author, year, title, journal, DOI) and are organized for reference and navigation rather than for developing a coherent idea or argument. It also excludes isolated numerical elements such as page numbers, which, despite being textually formatted, are functionally and semantically disconnected from the main narrative and do not contribute to the development of a coherent idea or argument. Crucially, it excludes any text that is organized as a list, series of questions, or bullet-pointed responses, even if it contains sentences, because such structures are segmented, functionally oriented toward interaction or guidance, and lack the continuous, flowing discourse that defines a true paragraph. It also excludes section titles, headings, and subheadings, even if they contain mathematical notation or are formatted in bold or a different font, because they serve as structural labels rather than narrative units and do not develop a coherent idea or argument through continuous prose. These structural elements are typically marked by distinct formatting (e.g., bold, numbered, or centered text) and serve to organize the document’s hierarchy, not to convey a continuous narrative or analytical development. A true paragraph maintains a continuous, flowing discourse without segmentation into discrete, non-continuous units, and is not organized in a tabular or list-like format, even if it contains sentences or references. The defining feature is the presence of a coherent, uninterrupted narrative or analytical development, regardless of the inclusion of numerical data, references, or citations, as long as the text flows continuously and develops a single idea or argument.'}]
reference text: [{'reference text': "A self-contained, structured citation for an academic source, appearing as a distinct entry in a bibliography or reference list. It begins with a numerical identifier, followed by the author's name (last name followed by initials), the publication year in parentheses, the title of the work in sentence case, and source information (e.g., journal name in italics, volume, issue, and page numbers for articles, or publisher and place for books). The entry is punctuated with periods to separate components and commas to separate multiple authors, forming a standardized, hierarchical format that enables the identification and retrieval of a specific scholarly work. This complete and formal structure clearly differentiates it from isolated author names, section headings, mathematical equations, or other non-citation text. A reference text does not contain mathematical symbols, variables, or formulas, and is not embedded within a paragraph of explanatory text as an equation or footnote would be. It is a standalone entry, not part of a larger paragraph or section of text, and is not a figure caption, table caption, or descriptive label for a figure or table. It is distinct from citations that appear within the main text, such as footnotes or endnotes, which are integrated into the narrative flow and are not formatted as complete reference list entries. A reference text is typically followed by a DOI (Digital Object Identifier) and is not part of a list of figures, tables, or other navigational elements, which may contain similar structured text but lack the full bibliographic details and DOI. Importantly, a reference text must include a complete bibliographic record for a scholarly work, including author, year, title, and source information, and must not be a section title, chapter heading, or table of contents entry, even if it is formatted with a page number. It must not be a standalone number that functions as a page indicator, such as a page number at the bottom of a document, which is a navigational element and not a bibliographic entry. Additionally, a reference text must not contain explanatory prose, in-text citations, or references to figures, and must not be embedded within a narrative paragraph or appear as part of a descriptive label or caption. A reference text is specifically a bibliographic entry, not a document title, section heading, or organizational label, and must be formatted as a complete, standalone citation with all required bibliographic components. It must not contain mathematical expressions, symbols, or variables, and must not be a section or subsection label, even if it includes a number and a title. Furthermore, a reference text is typically part of a list of multiple, similarly structured entries, each representing a distinct scholarly work, and is not a single, numbered heading that introduces a section of text. It must not be part of a table, even if it contains a complete citation, and must be a standalone entry in a reference list or bibliography. A reference text is fundamentally a bibliographic citation, not a navigational element like a table of contents, and must include a complete set of bibliographic components (author, year, title, source) that are absent in navigational lists. It is not a document title, dissertation title, or any other form of prominent heading that introduces a work or section, even if it is formatted with a number and a title. The object must be a list of such entries, not a single entry within a table or any other structured data format."}]
section: [{'section': 'A section heading is a structural element in a document that marks the beginning of a major, self-contained part of the text, such as a chapter, appendix, or main section. It is typically formatted in bold text, includes a descriptive title and a numerical or alphabetical label (e.g., "APPENDIX H"), and is positioned at the top of a page or at the start of a new content block. This heading functions as a clear organizational marker, introducing a new section and is followed by a substantial amount of text or other content. The heading is distinct from non-structural elements such as figure captions, equation labels, or references, which may be numbered or formatted similarly but do not serve as section dividers. It is also distinct from hierarchical subsections, which are typically numbered and nested within a larger section. Importantly, a section heading is not a numbered label or part of a formula, and it does not reference a mathematical expression or equation. Additionally, a section heading is not a footnote or endnote, which are supplementary elements marked by a superscript number and appear at the bottom of the page or within the text, providing commentary or citations rather than introducing a new section of content. Furthermore, a section heading is not a list title or a list of items, which are structural elements that organize a collection of related items rather than introducing a new section of content. It is also distinct from entries in a table of contents, which are navigational aids and do not introduce new content. A section heading is not a table entry or a data structure containing a list of textual entries with associated labels and data. The heading may also include a date or other contextual information, such as in an appendix, and is not part of a mathematical or technical proof. The heading may be centered or left-aligned, but it is always bolded and followed by a substantial amount of text. It is not followed by a table, figure, or other data structure, which are typically labeled by captions that are distinct from section headings. A section heading is not a title on a cover page or a title page, which is a standalone structural element that does not introduce a new section of content and is not followed by a substantial amount of text.'}]
subsection: [{'subsection': 'A structural heading that introduces a new, specific subsection within a document. It is characterized by a title in bold, capitalized, title-case text, preceded by a numerical hierarchical label (e.g., "3.3" or "6.2.1"). This heading is always followed by a block of descriptive, narrative text that elaborates on the topic introduced and is part of the main body of the document, distinguishing it from standalone elements such as page numbers, figure captions, footnotes, table of contents entries, and reference lists. The following text must be continuous, interpretive, and integrated into the narrative flow, discussing experimental results, implications, or contextual information, rather than technical or formulaic content. The heading must not be part of a table, figure, or other non-textual structure, and must be located within the main body of the document, not in navigational or structural elements like a table of contents or title page. It serves as a hierarchical organizational element that structures the document’s content.'}]
subsubsection: [{'subsubsection': 'A structural marker within the main body of a scholarly document that functions as a section heading, characterized by a bold sans-serif formatting and a descriptive title summarizing the section content. It is positioned at the beginning of a new section and serves to organize the document, distinguishing it from non-hierarchical text such as figure captions, table captions, or embedded mathematical equations. Subsubsections are part of a nested hierarchical structure and are typically smaller in scale compared to chapter or section headings, often appearing after a section heading and before a paragraph of text. The heading must be a standalone element at the start of a section, not embedded within a paragraph or associated with mathematical expressions, and must not be a label for equations or figures. It must be followed by a paragraph of text or a new section, ensuring it is a distinct structural element within the document hierarchy. It must not be part of a reference list or bibliography, which are non-hierarchical collections of citations, and must not resemble a bibliographic entry in structure or content. It must not be a content claim, such as a hypothesis or research statement, even if it is labeled with a number and appears in the main body of the text. Additionally, it must not be a title for a list, table of contents, or other non-hierarchical front-matter or back-matter elements, and must be part of a continuous, nested hierarchical structure within the main body of the document.'}]
table: [{'table': 'A table is a structured visual element organized in a grid of rows and columns, designed to present data in a systematic and comparative format. It features a header row defining column content and a first column listing categories or variables. The cells contain specific values—numerical or textual—that are arranged to allow direct comparison and analysis of relationships between variables. The table is typically labeled as "Table X" and appears as a distinct, isolated element, separate from the main narrative text flow. It is not embedded within paragraphs, nor does it function as a navigational aid, figure, chart, graph, or mathematical expression. The grid structure is clear and consistent, with data presented in a tabular layout rather than as a list, block of text, or hierarchical outline. Tables are distinguished from other document elements by their purpose—presenting measurable information for analysis—and their labeling convention, which uses "Table X" rather than "Figure X" or other designations.'}]
table caption: [{'table caption': 'A structured textual label positioned directly above a table, consisting of a table number (e.g., "Table X.Y") followed by a brief descriptive title that summarizes the table’s content and purpose. The label is formatted to stand out from surrounding text, typically using bold styling, centered alignment, and sometimes enclosed in a box or distinct layout. It serves as an introduction to the table, enabling readers to understand its content without referring to the main text. The caption is directly linked to the table it describes and must not be a standalone note, citation, methodological explanation, equation, reference, or definition. It is not a figure caption, section header, chapter title, thesis title, or part of a table of contents. It does not describe visual data such as charts, graphs, or images, nor does it include bibliographic entries or supplementary explanatory text. The primary function is to identify and introduce a table, and it must be spatially and semantically associated with the table it precedes.'}]
table of contents text: Entries in the table of contents listing sections and their page numbers.
title: [{'title': 'The primary identifier of an academic document, such as a thesis or dissertation, which is centrally positioned at the top of the first page, formatted in a large, bold, capitalized serif font, and enclosed within a rectangular border. It is the most visually dominant text on the page, clearly distinguishing the work from other content, and is typically followed by a subtitle, document type, and author information. The title is distinct from other text due to its size, boldness, central placement, and role as the main identifier of the entire document. It is not a section or chapter heading, nor is it a mathematical equation, formula, or a reference to a formula, nor is it a figure caption, table caption, or any other form of supplementary text. It is also not a numbered equation or a formula reference, even if it is labeled with a number, and it is not a text block that appears within the body of the document. The title is specifically the main identifier of the entire document and is not a heading for a list or section, such as a "List of Tables" or "List of Figures," even if it is formatted prominently. It is also not a list of section titles or a table of contents entry, regardless of its formatting or prominence. The title is not a page number or any other navigational element, and it is not located at the bottom of the page or in the body of the document. The title is exclusively the main identifier of the entire document and is not a text block that appears within the body of the document, even if it is formatted prominently. It is also not a section or subsection heading, even if it is bolded or numbered, and it is not part of the document\'s internal structural hierarchy. The title is not a title of a table, figure, or any other supplementary element, and it is not a component of a data presentation. The title is specifically the main identifier of the entire document and is not a label for a table, figure, or any other supplementary content, even if it is formatted prominently or appears in the body of the document.'}]
